name: The Waiting Room
description: A cavernous room furnished with rows of stone benches and a numbered display board reading 'NOW SERVING: 3.' Your ticket says 847. A translucent figure sits on one of the benches -- a ghost who appears to have been waiting for a very, very long time. The walls are covered in tally marks, motivational posters that have aged into irony, and one sign reading 'YOUR PATIENCE IS APPRECIATED (BUT NOT REQUIRED -- YOU HAVE NO CHOICE).'

responses.look: A stone display board reads 'NOW SERVING: 3.' It has read 'NOW SERVING: 3' for approximately two hundred years. The ghost on the bench confirms this. The queue system is, in a word, deliberate.
responses.use: You press buttons on the display. Nothing changes. The number 3 regards you with the permanence of a mathematical constant.
responses.default: You stare at the display. The display stares at the number 3. The number 3 stares into eternity.
responses.take: The number display is bolted to the wall and enchanted against removal. The Clerk's institutional paranoia extends to fixtures.
responses.talk: You address the number 3 directly. It does not respond. You are now the kind of person who talks to numbers. The waiting room has claimed another victim.
responses.open: The display has no door, no hatch, no secret panel. Whatever dark engine powers the queue, it was not designed to be understood.
responses.push: You push the display. The number flickers to 4 for one glorious, heart-stopping instant -- then snaps back to 3.
responses.pull: You pull at the display board. A small plaque underneath reads: 'TAMPERING WITH QUEUE INFRASTRUCTURE IS PUNISHABLE BY ADDITIONAL WAITING.'

responses.look: Stone benches worn smooth by centuries of waiting. Each one has a small brass plaque reading 'PLEASE REMAIN SEATED.' Some have tally marks scratched into them -- the longest set reaches 73,847. Someone was keeping count. They presumably stopped when they ran out of stone. They look comfortable. Suspiciously comfortable.
responses.default: You examine the benches. They've hosted more patience than any piece of furniture should have to bear.
responses.take: You attempt to lift a stone bench. It weighs approximately the same as your remaining will to live, which is to say: far too much.
responses.use: You sit on the stone bench. It is exactly as comfortable as sitting on a stone bench. A brass plaque reads 'PLEASE REMAIN SEATED.' It wasn't a suggestion.
responses.talk: You whisper to the bench: 'How do you do it? How do you just... sit here?' The bench does not answer. It has mastered patience through the simple expedient of having no nervous system.
responses.open: There is nothing to open on a stone bench. You check anyway, because you are in a waiting room with no end.
responses.push: You shove the bench. It doesn't move. It has been here longer than most civilisations and has no intention of going anywhere.
responses.pull: You pull at the bench. It is fused to the floor by time, magic, or the sheer accumulated weight of boredom. Probably all three.

responses.look: 'HANG IN THERE!' reads one poster, featuring a cat clinging to a branch. The poster is 400 years old. The cat is long dead. The irony writes itself. Another reads 'GOOD THINGS COME TO THOSE WHO WAIT' in a font that suggests the author was being sarcastic.
responses.use: You read the posters more carefully. One, half-hidden behind the others, reads: 'For faster service, seek VIP authorization. Check the filing room.' This feels like a hint. Or a trap. In the Screaming Caverns, the distinction is academic.
responses.default: You peruse the motivational posters. They motivate nothing except existential dread.
responses.take: You peel a poster from the wall. Underneath is another poster. Underneath that, another. It's posters all the way down.
responses.talk: You read the posters aloud, as though they might hear themselves and feel ashamed. 'Hang in there.' The words echo off stone. The posters remain unrepentant.
responses.open: These are posters, not doors. Though at this point, you'd try opening a poster if it meant getting out of this queue.
responses.push: You push the posters flat against the wall. They spring back, curled at the edges, defiant. Four hundred years of ageing has given them structural opinions.
responses.pull: You pull a corner of one poster. It tears, revealing an inscription on the wall: 'Gerald was here. Day 12,045.' Below it: 'Gerald left. Day 12,046. Nobody noticed.'

responses.look: Rows of uncomfortable chairs, designed by someone who understood that waiting should be unpleasant on every possible level.
responses.take: They're bolted to the floor. Even the furniture is trapped here.
responses.use: You sit briefly. The cushion has the structural integrity of a broken promise.
responses.default: You eye the waiting chairs. They offer rest, technically. Comfort, not at all.
responses.talk: You address the chairs. They creak. Whether this is a response or the natural complaint of furniture that has supported anxious bureaucratic petitioners for centuries is unclear.
responses.open: The chairs are solid wood and iron. There's nothing to open. They were designed for one purpose: making you uncomfortable while you wait. They excel at this.
responses.push: You shove a chair. It scrapes across the stone floor with a shriek that echoes through the waiting room. Several imaginary petitioners give you disapproving looks.
responses.pull: You pull a chair toward you. It resists, its legs catching on every crack in the stone floor. Even the furniture here is uncooperative.

responses.look: A glowing display shows the current serving number. It hasn't changed in what appears to be centuries. The queue moves at geological speed.
responses.take: It's mounted to the wall with magical bindings. Not even theft can speed up this process.
responses.use: You stare at it, willing the number to change. It does not change. Bureaucracy wins again.
responses.default: You regard the number board. It radiates the quiet authority of a system that will never be rushed.
responses.talk: You address the number board. It clicks to the next number with mechanical indifference. Whether it heard you or simply advanced on schedule is a question for philosophers, not adventurers.
responses.open: The number board is a flat mechanical display bolted to the wall. There's no panel to remove, no back to open. Its inner workings are as inaccessible as the Clerk's emotional range.
responses.push: You push the number board. It clicks forward one number. Then back. Then forward three. The mechanism has opinions about being manhandled, and it expresses them numerically.
responses.pull: You pull at the number board. It's bolted to the wall with fasteners that were installed by someone who anticipated frustrated petitioners. The bolts hold. The board judges.

responses.look: Your queue ticket reads 847 in faded ink. The paper is thin, slightly warm, and vibrates faintly as though aware of its own insignificance. Below the number, in microscopic print: 'Your patience is mandatory.'
responses.take: You already have it. It's in your hand. It's been in your hand since you arrived. Letting go of it would be like letting go of hope -- technically possible but practically unthinkable.
responses.use: You hold up the ticket. Nothing happens. Queue position is non-negotiable, non-transferable, and apparently non-functional.
responses.talk: You whisper to the ticket: 'When will it be our turn?' The ticket, being paper, offers no comfort. Being number 847 when they're serving 3 is comfort enough.
responses.open: It's a slip of paper. It's already as open as paper gets. What were you expecting inside -- a smaller, more optimistic ticket?
responses.push: You press the ticket flat. It was already flat. Paper tends to be. Your contribution to its flatness is negligible.
responses.pull: You tug at the ticket's edges. The paper stretches slightly but doesn't tear. It's sturdier than it looks, which is the minimum requirement for something that has to outlast bureaucracy.

responses.look: Thousands of tally marks are scratched into the walls, layered over each other in increasingly desperate handwriting. The earliest ones are neat and orderly. The later ones are wild, carved deep into the stone with what appears to be fingernails. One section reads 'Gerald was here. Day 12,045. Still waiting.' Below it, in different handwriting: 'Gerald left. Day 12,046. Nobody noticed.'
responses.take: You can't take scratches out of a wall. They're part of the wall now, the way despair is part of the waiting experience.
responses.use: You add your own tally mark to the wall. It looks small and hopeful next to the thousands already there. Give it time.
responses.talk: You read the tally marks aloud. The numbers are staggering. Someone waited twelve thousand days. Someone else gave up at forty. You feel seen.
responses.open: Tally marks don't open. They're scratches. They tell a story, but not the kind with a door in it.
responses.push: You press your hand against the tally marks. The scratches are deep enough to feel under your fingers. Each one represents someone who believed their wait would end.
responses.pull: You trace the deepest scratches with your fingernails. Someone carved these with desperate force. The wall endured it all, patient as bureaucracy itself.

boredom-death.title: Death by Patience
boredom-death.narratorText: You sit down to wait. And wait. And wait. Time passes. Seasons change above ground. Empires rise and fall. The number display remains at 3. Your body slows, your breathing shallows, and eventually you simply... stop. The ghost nearby pats your shoulder sympathetically. 'Don't worry,' he says. 'You get used to it. After the first century.' Your skeleton continues to wait. It is very patient now.

responses.look: A spectral figure clutches a faded ticket numbered 4. It has been waiting since before the curse. Before the kingdom, possibly. Its expression combines hope, despair, and the quiet madness of someone who has been in a queue for centuries.
responses.talk: 'Is it my turn yet?' the ghost whispers. You check the display. Now serving: 3. The ghost has been one number away for an eternity. You lack the heart to tell it that number 3 appears to have left.
responses.use: You cannot use a ghost. The ghost cannot use itself. Nobody in this waiting room is being used productively. That appears to be the point.
responses.take: The ghost looks at you with hollow eyes. 'You can't take my place in the queue. I've waited too long.' It clutches its ticket tighter.
responses.default: The queue ghost stares at the number display with the hope of someone who has been disappointed every second for three hundred years but refuses to give up.
